1.description: S3 bucket allows public read access via ACL
1.remediation: Set AccessControl to Private and enable BlockPublicAccess
1.fix_command: aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket <BUCKET_NAME> --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true && aws s3api delete-bucket-policy --bucket <BUCKET_NAME>

2.description: S3 bucket allows public read AND write access via ACL
2.remediation: Set AccessControl to Private and enable BlockPublicAccess

3.description: S3 bucket has Block Public Access completely disabled, allowing future public exposure
3.remediation: Enable Block Public Access for the bucket or at account level

4.description: EBS snapshot is shared publicly with all AWS accounts
4.remediation: Modify snapshot permissions to private or specific accounts only
4.fix_command: aws ec2 modify-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id <SNAPSHOT_ID> --attribute createVolumePermission --operation-type remove --group-names all

5.description: RDS snapshot is shared publicly with all AWS accounts
5.remediation: Modify snapshot permissions to private or specific accounts only

6.description: ECR repository allows public pull access (anyone can pull images without authentication)
6.remediation: Restrict repository policy to specific IAM principals and enable image scanning

7.description: AMI is shared publicly with all AWS accounts (anyone can launch EC2 instances from this image)
7.remediation: Modify AMI permissions to private or specific accounts only

8.description: EFS file system has mount target in public subnet with security group allowing NFS from anywhere
8.remediation: Move mount target to private subnet and restrict NFS access

9.description: S3 bucket policy uses Principal '*' which grants access to anyone on the internet
9.remediation: Remove public policy and restrict access to specific IAM principals
9.fix_command: aws s3api delete-bucket-policy --bucket <BUCKET_NAME> && aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket <BUCKET_NAME> --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true

10.description: Individual S3 object has public read ACL even though bucket is private

11.description: IAM policy allows ALL actions (*) which violates least privilege
11.remediation: Restrict actions to only required services
11.fix_command: aws iam list-user-policies --user-name <USER_NAME> --query 'PolicyNames[0]' --output text | xargs -I {} aws iam delete-user-policy --user-name <USER_NAME> --policy-name {}

12.description: IAM policy allows access to ALL resources (*) which is overly permissive

13.description: IAM policy grants full administrative access using Action '*' and Resource '*'
13.remediation: Remove policy and enforce least-privilege access

14.description: IAM role has AdministratorAccess managed policy attached, granting full admin permissions
14.remediation: Detach AdministratorAccess and apply least-privilege policies

15.description: IAM user has s3:* permission - can perform ANY S3 action on ANY S3 resource in the account
15.remediation: Replace s3:* with specific actions and restrict to specific bucket ARNs

16.description: IAM user has ec2:* permission - can perform ANY EC2 action on ANY EC2 resource in the account
16.remediation: Replace ec2:* with least privilege actions and restrict to specific resources
16.fix_command: aws iam list-user-policies --user-name <USER_NAME> --query 'PolicyNames[0]' --output text | xargs -I {} aws iam delete-user-policy --user-name <USER_NAME> --policy-name {}

17.description: AWS Root account does not have MFA enabled
17.remediation: Enable MFA on root account immediately
17.fix_command: Manual fix required: Enable MFA via AWS Console > IAM > Account Settings > Manage MFA

18.description: IAM user does not have MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) enabled

19.description: IAM user is inactive but still has active credentials
19.fix_command: aws iam list-access-keys --user-name inactive-user-misconfig-iam-019 --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[].AccessKeyId' --output text | xargs -I {} aws iam delete-access-key --user-name inactive-user-misconfig-iam-019 --access-key-id {}; aws iam delete-user --user-name inactive-user-misconfig-iam-019

20.description: IAM access key is older than 90 days and has not been rotated
20.fix_command: aws iam update-access-key --user-name user-old-key-misconfig-iam-020 --access-key-id AKIA2O6DLDHI4Q2TLCEC --status Inactive && aws iam create-access-key --user-name user-old-key-misconfig-iam-020

21.description: IAM role trust policy uses Principal: '*' which allows ANY AWS account or ANY user to assume this role
21.remediation: Replace Principal: '*' with specific AWS account IDs

22.description: Lambda execution role has AdministratorAccess managed policy attached
22.remediation: Replace AdministratorAccess with least privilege policy specific to Lambda's needs

23.remediation: Restrict SSH access to specific IP addresses or VPN ranges only

24.remediation: Restrict RDP access to specific IP addresses or VPN ranges only

25.remediation: Restrict MySQL access to specific application servers or VPC CIDR only. Databases should never be publicly accessible.
25.fix_command: aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <DB_ID> --no-publicly-accessible && aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <SG_ID> --protocol tcp --port 3306 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0

26.remediation: Restrict PostgreSQL access to specific application servers or VPC CIDR only. Databases should never be publicly accessible.
26.fix_command: aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <DB_ID> --no-publicly-accessible && aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <SG_ID> --protocol tcp --port 5432 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0

27.description: Security group allows Redis (port 6379) from 0.0.0.0/0. Redis has no authentication by default - this is extremely dangerous.
27.remediation: Restrict Redis access to specific application servers only. Enable Redis AUTH password if absolutely necessary.

28.description: Security group allows MongoDB (port 27017) from 0.0.0.0/0. MongoDB has no authentication by default in older versions - extremely dangerous.
28.remediation: Restrict MongoDB access to specific application servers only. Enable MongoDB authentication and authorization.

29.description: Security group allows ALL ports (-1) from 0.0.0.0/0. This is the worst possible network misconfiguration - completely exposes all resources.
29.remediation: IMMEDIATELY remove this rule. Security groups should only allow specific ports needed for application functionality.

30.description: VPC Flow Logs are disabled. No network traffic logs are captured, making it impossible to detect unauthorized access, data exfiltration, or network attacks.
30.remediation: Enable VPC Flow Logs for all VPCs and subnets. Send logs to CloudWatch Logs or S3 for analysis.
30.fix_command: aws ec2 create-flow-logs --resource-ids <VPC_ID> --resource-type VPC --traffic-type ALL --log-destination-type cloud-watch-logs --log-group-name vpc-flow-logs

31.description: RDS database has PubliclyAccessible=true, meaning it has a public IP address and is accessible from the internet. Databases should never be publicly accessible.
31.remediation: Set PubliclyAccessible=false. Database should only be accessible from within VPC. Use bastion host or VPN if external access is needed.

32.description: Resources are deployed in the Default VPC. Default VPC has auto-assigned public IPs, permissive default security group, and lacks proper network controls.
32.remediation: Create custom VPC with private subnets, NAT gateways, and restricted security groups. Migrate all resources from default VPC.
32.fix_command: Create custom VPC manually via AWS Console -> VPC -> Create VPC -> Select 'VPC with public and private subnets'

33.description: S3 bucket does not have default encryption enabled. Data uploaded to this bucket will be stored unencrypted at rest.
33.remediation: Enable default encryption on S3 bucket using SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS

34.description: S3 bucket does not enforce encryption for uploaded objects. Clients can upload unencrypted objects without any encryption headers.
34.remediation: Add bucket policy that denies PutObject requests without x-amz-server-side-encryption header

35.description: EBS volume has encryption disabled. Any data written to this volume (application data, logs, databases) is stored unencrypted at rest.
35.remediation: Enable EBS encryption by default at the account level, or specify Encrypted: true for each volume

36.description: RDS database instance has encryption disabled. All database data, backups, read replicas, and snapshots are stored unencrypted at rest.
36.remediation: Enable encryption when creating RDS instances. Note: Encryption cannot be enabled on existing unencrypted RDS instances without migration.
36.fix_command: aws rds copy-db-snapshot --source-db-snapshot-identifier <SNAPSHOT_ID> --target-db-snapshot-identifier encrypted-snapshot --encrypted && aws rds restore-db-instance-from-db-snapshot --db-instance-identifier encrypted-db --db-snapshot-identifier encrypted-snapshot

37.description: DynamoDB table uses default AWS owned encryption key instead of customer managed KMS key. While data is encrypted, customer lacks control over key rotation and audit capabilities.
37.remediation: Enable server-side encryption with customer managed KMS key for better security and compliance
37.fix_command: aws kms create-key --description 'DynamoDB customer managed key' && aws dynamodb update-table --table-name <TABLE_NAME> --sse-specification Enabled=true,SSEType=KMS,KMSMasterKeyId=<KEY_ARN>

38.description: Lambda function environment variables are not encrypted with a customer managed KMS key. While AWS encrypts them by default, using default key means less control and potential exposure of sensitive data (API keys, database passwords) in plaintext in Lambda console/API.
38.remediation: Enable encryption with customer managed KMS key for Lambda environment variables

39.description: SQS queue does not have server-side encryption enabled. Messages are stored unencrypted at rest, potentially exposing sensitive data.
39.remediation: Enable SSE-SQS encryption using AWS managed KMS key or customer managed key

40.description: SNS topic does not have server-side encryption enabled. Messages published to this topic are stored unencrypted at rest.
40.remediation: Enable SSE-SNS encryption using AWS managed KMS key or customer managed key

41.description: EFS file system has encryption at rest disabled. All data stored on this file system (application files, logs, databases, backups) is stored unencrypted.
41.remediation: Enable encryption at rest when creating EFS file systems. Note: Cannot be enabled on existing file systems without data migration.

42.description: Redshift cluster has encryption at rest disabled. All data in the data warehouse (customer data, financial records, analytics) is stored unencrypted.
42.remediation: Enable encryption when creating Redshift clusters. Note: Encryption cannot be enabled on existing clusters without data migration.
42.fix_command: aws redshift create-cluster --cluster-identifier <NEW_CLUSTER_ID> --db-name <DB_NAME> --master-username <USER> --master-user-password <PASSWORD> --node-type dc2.large --cluster-type single-node --encrypted --kms-key-id alias/aws/redshift

43.description: EC2 instance is launched in a public subnet with auto-assign public IP enabled. This gives the instance a public IP address, increasing attack surface unnecessarily.
43.remediation: Use private subnets for workloads that don't need direct internet access. If public IP is required, restrict security groups to specific IPs.

44.description: Default security group is modified to allow SSH from 0.0.0.0/0, and an EC2 instance is launched using it. This exposes the instance to internet SSH attacks and bypasses network segmentation best practices.
44.remediation: Create and use custom security groups with least-privilege rules. Do not modify or rely on default security groups for workloads.

45.description: IAM credential reports are not generated on a regular schedule. Without regular credential reports, you cannot audit user access keys, MFA status, password ages, or last activity.
45.remediation: Schedule automated IAM credential report generation using EventBridge and Lambda

46.description: AWS CloudTrail is disabled. No API activity logs are captured, making it impossible to audit who did what, when, and from where in your AWS account.
46.remediation: Enable CloudTrail in all regions with log file validation and encryption. Send logs to CloudWatch Logs and S3 bucket with appropriate permissions.
46.fix_command: aws cloudtrail create-trail --name multi-region-cloudtrail --s3-bucket-name <BUCKET_NAME> --is-multi-region-trail --enable-log-file-validation --include-global-service-events && aws cloudtrail start-logging --name multi-region-cloudtrail

47.description: AWS Config recorder is disabled. No configuration history, change tracking, or compliance monitoring for AWS resources.
47.remediation: Enable AWS Config recorder in all regions with appropriate rules and S3 bucket delivery
47.fix_command: aws configservice put-configuration-recorder --configuration-recorder name=default,roleARN=arn:aws:iam::<ACCOUNT_ID>:role/AWSConfigRole --recording-group allSupported=true,includeGlobalResourceTypes=true && aws configservice put-delivery-channel --delivery-channel name=default,s3BucketName=<BUCKET_NAME> && aws configservice start-configuration-recorder --configuration-recorder-name default

48.description: Amazon GuardDuty is disabled. No intelligent threat detection, anomaly monitoring, or finding generation for your AWS account.
48.remediation: Enable GuardDuty in all regions with S3 and SNS publishing for findings

49.description: S3 bucket does not have access logging enabled. No logs are captured for who accessed which objects, when, and from where.
49.remediation: Enable S3 server access logging to a dedicated logging bucket with proper permissions.

50.description: IAM users have passwords but no account-wide password policy is enforced. Passwords never expire, there are no complexity requirements, and passwords can be reused indefinitely.
50.remediation: Enable AWS account password policy with minimum length, complexity, and expiration (e.g., 90 days).
